title: Valve Authority Range for HVAC Coil Applications
summary: Authorities in the range of 30 to 50% are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority.
evidence_quote: Authorities in the range of 30 to 50% (for piping systems with 2:1 branch-to-riser pressure drop ratios) are adequate for HVAC coil applications, with higher Δt coils requiring less valve authority.

title: Minimum Valve Authority
summary: Authorities less than 30% should be avoided because they lead to a flow characteristic shift.
evidence_quote: Authorities less than 30% should be avoided because they lead to a flow characteristic shift that makes the equal-percentage valve appear to be more linear in nature.

title: Control Valve Pressure Drop for Complementary Characteristic
summary: In variable-speed pumping with differential pressure control, the valve pressure drop for control is that required to provide complementary characteristic.
evidence_quote: The valve pressure drop for control, then, is that required to provide complementary characteristic.

title: Valve Authority for Good Results with 6.7 K Δt Coil
summary: For coils with a 6.7 K Δt, valve authority of 50 to 100% provides good results, implying a pressure drop on the valve equal to that of the coil.
evidence_quote: In the previous coils, a 6.7 K Δt was used, and valve authority of 50 to 100% provides good results, implying a pressure drop on the valve equal to that of the coil.

title: Low-Temperature Heating System Supply Temperatures
summary: Low-temperature heating systems are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.
evidence_quote: They are generally designed with supply water temperatures from 80 to 115°C and temperature drops from 10 to 55 K.

title: Historical US Hot-Water Heating Design Temperatures
summary: Historically, US hot-water heating systems were designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.
evidence_quote: In the United States, hot-water heating systems were historically designed for a 93°C supply water temperature and an 11 K temperature drop.

title: Coil Rating Parameters per AHRI Standard 410
summary: Dry-surface and dehumidifying coils are rated within parameters including entering air temperatures, air face velocity, and water velocity.
evidence_quote:
Entering air dry-bulb temperature: 18 to 38°C
Entering air wet-bulb temperature: 15 to 30°C
Air face velocity: 1 to 4 m/s
Entering chilled-water temperature: 1.5 to 18°C
Water velocity: 0.3 to 2.4 m/s

title: Dehumidifying Coil Air Face Velocity Selection Range
summary: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.
evidence_quote: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.

title: Standard Air Density for Airflow Ratings
summary: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.
evidence_quote: Airflow ratings are based on standard air of 1.20 kg/m³ at 20°C and a barometric pressure of 101.325 kPa.

title: Coil Freezing Threshold Refrigerant Temperature
summary: Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.
evidence_quote: Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated; the exact value depends on the design of the coil, its operating dew point, and the amount of loading.

title: Airflow Resistance Difference Between Dry and Dehumidifying Coils
summary: A completely dry coil, removing only sensible heat, offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.
evidence_quote: A completely dry coil, removing only sensible heat, offers approximately one-third less resistance to airflow than a dehumidifying coil removing both sensible and latent heat.

title: Hydrophilic Coating Air Pressure Drop Reduction
summary: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil.
evidence_quote: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil.
